content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 21, 1990


Jeffrey J. Carter
Treasurer
Save Affordable Housing Committee
1440 Broadway, Suite 1000
Oakland, California  94612

                                    Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                         Our File No. A-90-746

Dear Mr. Carter:

     This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you concerning the propriety of using
the remaining campaign funds of the Save Affordable Housing
Committee to make a contribution to the Berkeley Tenant's Union
under the new "personal use" provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1/

     This letter confirms that your letter dated December 10,
1990, accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you
on that date. As I stated in our telephone conversation, Section
85800 of the Act provides:

          (a)  This article applies to campaign funds
held by candidates for elective office, elected
officers, controlled committees, ballot measure
committees, committees opposed to a candidate or
measure, political action committees, and any
committee which qualifies as a committee pursuant
to subdivision (a) of Section 82013.

          (b)  (1) For purposes of this chapter,
"campaign funds" includes any contributions, cash,
cash equivalents, and other assets received or .


_____________________________
1/   Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.  Sec-
tions 85800-85807, which describe the permissible use of campaign
funds, are referred to as the "personal use" laws.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-746
Page 2


possessed by a committee as defined by subdivision
(a) of Section 82013.

[Emphasis added.]

The general rule of the new personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 85801.) However, where an expenditure confers a
substantial personal benefit on the candidate or other controlling
person, the expenditure must be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose.2/ (Section 85801.)

Moreover, where campaign funds become surplus campaign funds,
the personal use law further limits their expenditures. (Section
85807; Regulation 18587, copy enclosed.) However, Section 85807
expressly applies the definition of "surplus funds" to candidates
and their committees only.3/ Thus, campaign funds held by measure
committees do not become surplus funds and may be disposed of in a
manner consistent with Government Code Sections 85800 through
85806.

You asked whether campaign funds held by a committee opposed
to a ballot measure, after the election on the Ballots measure,
may be used to contribute to the Berkeley Tenants' Union. You
stated the Berkeley Tenants' Union has goals similar to those
pursued by the committee.

As stated above, if the expenditure confers a substantial
personal benefit on any person involved in the decisionmaking
process of the committee, the expenditure is permitted only if it
is directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.4/ (Section 85801.) However, absent such a personal
benefit, the committee may make any expenditure of campaign funds
which is reasonably related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. As I stated in our telephone conversation,
the transfer appears to be reasonably related to a political
purpose and would therefore be permissible.


2/ A "substantial personal benefit" is defined as a direct
personal benefit to the candidate of $100 or more. (Section
85800(b)(2); Regulation 18580, copy enclosed.)

3/ Other sections of the personal use law have been interpreted
more broadly to apply to committees and candidates. The surplus
funds provision, however, has historically been interpreted to
apply to candidates and their controlled committees only.

4/ For example, if the money transferred into the Union was to be
used to pay a salary of $100 to a person who was also a
controlling member of the committee, the direct standard would be
applicable.

Our File No. A-90-746
Page 3


     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                    Sincerely,

                                    Scott Hallabrin
                                    Acting General Counsel

                                    By: John W. Wallace
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

Enclosure
